basis_note: All dollar figures are directional proxies (unit_price x weekly appearance count for revenue; unit_cost x count for COGS). They show direction and relative size, not booked revenue. 30% of transactions are missing location metadata, so per-store figures are partial.

executive_summary.headline: Soft, steady week: traffic down ~3.7%, margin holding at ~63% -- but two staples (Cheese, Rice) likely stocked out and half your baskets are being discounted for zero lift.
executive_summary.the_60_second_read: Demand cooled across the board (transactions 4,468 -> 4,301, -3.7%; units -3.9%) -- this is broad traffic softness, not one bad SKU. Blended gross margin held firm at 63.2% on ~$146.9K revenue proxy / ~$92.8K gross profit. Two things matter most this week: (1) Cheese (-42%) and Rice (-35%) didn't lose demand, they almost certainly stocked out -- check receiving today and recover the volume. (2) You're discounting ~49% of baskets with no measurable lift, which is a live margin leak. Underneath, the business leans on 5 durable-goods SKUs for half its gross profit, so the real growth lever is shifting mix toward repeat-purchase, high-margin consumables. Next PO is smaller and more selective at ~$8,176 at cost: load the dozen confirmed risers led by Diapers (+29%), and HOLD Cheese and Rice until you know why they cratered.
executive_summary.strategic_narrative: Ordering and margin tell one coherent story this week. The reorder list and the margin map line up cleanly: the items you want to buy more of (Diapers, Chips, Dish Soap, Shampoo) are also healthy-margin, so leaning into them grows demand AND profit. The one place they conflict is Shrimp -- ordering says reorder it (+13.7%), margin says it's a thin 54% perishable -- and the synthesis answer is to do both: reorder to demand AND price-test it up to $17.99. The two anomaly drops (Cheese, Rice) are an operations problem, not a demand or pricing problem, and they should be the first phone call Monday. The bigger structural truth behind a 'steady' headline is revenue volatility: 5 durable SKUs carry 51% of gross profit, so the durable shelf must never stock out, and the long game is converting traffic into high-margin consumable baskets.

ordering_insights.summary: A disciplined, smaller PO into a softening week (~$8,176 at cost vs a broader buy last week). Concentrate spend on confirmed risers, override the rule to reorder Diapers, and hold the two anomaly drops. 15 order lines totaling ~$8,176.
ordering_insights.headline_signal: Diapers +29.46% WoW (129 -> 167) -- the week's single biggest mover. The reorder rule missed it by 1 unit (167 vs 168 cutoff) and the spike flag by 0.54pp (29.46% vs 30%). Both are threshold artifacts, not weak demand. Override and reorder; it's also the 6th-highest revenue SKU and a healthy 63% margin.

buy_more.rationale: Biggest mover of the week and high-ticket Baby item ($22.99) -- stockouts unacceptable. Margin-healthy momentum buy.
Strongest trend in the confirmed reorder list and high-margin household staple; cheap to stock deep.
Top-quartile velocity, clear upward consumable, healthy margin.
Reliable personal-care repeat purchase, strong margin.
Highest velocity in the confirmed list (188 units) and top revenue proxy ($2,442).
Best margin in the reorder set (70%) AND growing -- a flat-out winner, stock deep and expand facings.
Near-miss climber (2 units under velocity cutoff), strong pantry trend.
Reorder to demand only -- thin margin and perishable. See cross-report flag: pair the reorder with a price test.
Right at the trend threshold; modest top-up.

maintain.note: #1 volume SKU and basket anchor despite a pullback -- last week's 332 looks like a stock-up spike normalizing. Keep stocked, don't chase the peak, but investigate the dip (it's a traffic driver).
2nd-highest velocity, strong steady seller -- keep shelves full.

hold_or_trim.note: Do not reorder to prior levels until cause is known. Below-average margin makes over-ordering this perishable doubly risky.
Investigate before reordering -- sharp drop on a shelf-stable staple smells like a stockout, not lost demand.

hold_or_trim.product: Soft decliners (Iron -25.7%, Soap -20.1%, Insect Repellent -20.2%, Cereal -19.7%, Yogurt -19.3%, Hand Sanitizer -19.0%, BBQ Sauce -18.6%, Bread -18.3%)
hold_or_trim.note: Trim reorder quantities to avoid dead stock on a soft week; revisit next week.

ordering_insights.seasonal_watch: Insect Repellent (-20.2%) and Plant Fertilizer (-16.4%) faded despite heading into summer -- counter-seasonal and unexpected. Monitor one more week before adjusting seasonal buys; Lawn Mower roughly flat (-2.8%).

margin_insights.summary: Blended margin held at 63.2% -- but margins are engineered into a tight ~58-70% band for most SKUs, which means the handful of sub-56% items are the real, identifiable margin leaks where cost inflation has outrun shelf price. Dairy is the category to fix first: lowest margin (54.8%) AND shrinking across every SKU.

margin_leaks_cost_inflation.fix: Reprice to $4.79-$4.99 and/or re-bid egg supply. The clear outlier -- 6.7pts below the next-worst SKU and ~17pts below the 63% house average. $4.79 alone lifts margin to ~50%.
Test $6.29; review supplier cost. Watch for seasonal pickup into summer before discounting.
Demand is up +13.7% -- price-test to $17.99 (-> ~57% margin); the trend says it won't dent volume.
Move to $10.99 (-> ~59%). Market-wide olive-oil inflation makes the increase defensible and low-risk on a price-inelastic premium item.
Availability problem dwarfs the pricing problem this week (-42% volume). Verify stock first, revisit price only after volume recovers.
Modest price test to $15.49 given steady +5.5% demand; monitor against Shrimp cross-shift.

high_margin_winners_to_grow.note: Best-in-class: 70% margin AND +12% growth. Expand facings and add checkout/impulse placement.
#1 volume at 68% margin -- a basket anchor and traffic driver; protect it and investigate the 16% dip.
Highest-margin categories (Water 79.8%, Soda 73.2%, Chips 69.9%) but among the smallest in revenue. Front-of-store impulse merchandising lifts blended margin with little price resistance.
68% margin on only ~$2K revenue -- under-monetized; promote fresh-produce baskets to shift mix toward high-margin repeat purchases.

structural_risk.revenue_concentration: 5 durable-goods SKUs (Lawn Mower, Vacuum Cleaner, Ironing Board, Iron, Garden Hose) = ~$47.0K of ~$92.8K gross profit (51%) and ~50% of revenue. All are one-time, non-repeat purchases. This is revenue VOLATILITY risk, not a margin-mix problem -- the rest of the catalog still runs ~63% margin.
structural_risk.implication: A single Lawn Mower or Vacuum stockout hits the P&L harder than any consumable. Protect durable-goods availability above all, and de-risk over time by growing repeat-purchase consumable baskets.
structural_risk.lawn_mower_note: Lawn Mower alone is 24% of revenue proxy and 24% of gross profit. The 'Seasonal' category is essentially this one SKU.

margin_insights.promotion_leak: Nearly half of transactions carry a discount (2,121 of 4,301 = 49.3%), yet discounted baskets average $52.93 in COGS vs $51.74 for undiscounted -- discounted baskets are 2.3% LARGER, not smaller. There's no evidence the promo drives incremental lift; margin is being given away to customers who were buying anyway. (Caveat: the feed exposes basket COGS only, so exact giveaway can't be sized, but the direction is wrong.) Fix: replace blanket discounts with threshold/targeted offers and measure against incremental REVENUE, not cost.

combined_recommendations.ordering_signal: Cheese -42% and Rice -35% flagged as anomaly drops; HOLD reorders.
combined_recommendations.margin_implication: Both reports agree the drops read as stockouts, not demand loss. Cheese is also low-margin (55.7%), so the loss is an operational miss, not a pricing one.
combined_recommendations.combined_action: Check Cheese and Rice availability/receiving/spoilage TODAY. Recover the volume before adjusting either price or reorder quantity.

combined_recommendations.theme: Buy the winners, and they're high-margin too
combined_recommendations.ordering_signal: Diapers (+29%), Chips (+12%), Dish Soap (+21%), Shampoo (+14%) lead the reorder list.
combined_recommendations.margin_implication: These risers also carry 63-70% margins -- demand momentum and profit momentum point the same way.
combined_recommendations.combined_action: Load the full PO on these risers. Specifically expand Chips facings/checkout placement (70% margin + growth) and protect Diapers stock with Baby Wipes attach.

combined_recommendations.theme: Shrimp: reorder AND reprice (the one cross-signal conflict)
combined_recommendations.margin_implication: Only 54.09% margin and perishable -- a margin leak the ordering side wants to buy more of.
combined_recommendations.combined_action: Reorder to demand only (don't overbuy a perishable), and pair it with a price test to $17.99 (-> ~57% margin). Rising demand means the increase is unlikely to dent volume.

combined_recommendations.theme: Fix Dairy at the category level
combined_recommendations.ordering_signal: Cheese -42%, Yogurt -19%, plus broad dairy softness.
combined_recommendations.margin_implication: Dairy is the LOWEST-margin category (54.8%) AND shrinking across every SKU -- double jeopardy.
combined_recommendations.combined_action: Renegotiate dairy supply as a category rather than fixing six SKUs one at a time. Reprice Eggs ($4.49 -> $4.79+) as the standout outlier.

combined_recommendations.ordering_signal: Promotions are accretive to ticket on the surface (+2.3% basket).
combined_recommendations.margin_implication: But ~49% of baskets are discounted with no measurable incremental lift -- you're rewarding buyers who'd purchase anyway.
combined_recommendations.combined_action: Convert blanket discounts to threshold/targeted offers (unlock above a basket size, or target lapsing customers / slow-moving high-margin SKUs) and instrument against incremental revenue.

combined_recommendations.ordering_signal: Spend is concentrated in consumables; durables aren't in the reorder cycle but carry the P&L.
combined_recommendations.margin_implication: 5 durable SKUs = 51% of gross profit; a single durable stockout outweighs any consumable.
combined_recommendations.combined_action: Guard durable-goods availability above all, and grow repeat-purchase margin by mix-shifting toward Beverage (76%), Snacks (70%) and Produce (68%) via front-of-store merchandising.

anomalies_and_alerts.rule: Hard flag at abs(WoW) > 30%; spike if positive, drop if negative. Near-misses called out manually.

items.detail: 195 -> 113 units. A dairy staple doesn't lose 42% organically in a week -- strongly suggests stockout/supply gap/spoilage write-off. HIGHEST PRIORITY: check availability today. Both reports agree: hold reorder until cause is known.
184 -> 119 units. Same pattern on a shelf-stable staple -- reads as out-of-stock or supplier miss, not demand. Verify on-hand. Note: Rice is miscategorized as 'Bakery' in the feed.
129 -> 167, biggest gainer of the week. Missed the +30% spike flag by 0.54pp AND the reorder velocity cutoff by 1 unit -- treat as a de-facto spike and reorder. Capture attach sales (Baby Wipes).
187 -> 139. Below the 30% alert line but notable on a high-revenue durable ($5,559, 4th-largest gross profit). Confirm it's demand, not availability, given how much profit appliances carry.

anomalies_and_alerts.no_positive_spikes_over_threshold: No SKU crossed +30% this week; Diapers (+29.46%) was the closest.

location_performance.summary: Among named stores, basket economics are nearly uniform ($51.43-$53.53 COGS) -- there's no standout strong or weak store on the data available. The real story is a data gap: ~30% of transactions can't be attributed to any store, which blinds true location P&L.

named_stores.note: Richest basket among named stores (highest avg COGS).
Lowest avg basket cost, consistent with the warehouse-club format.
Lowest volume and near-lowest basket cost -- softest named store this week.

critical_data_gap.issue: 1,312 transactions (30.5% of all 4,301) have NO location metadata -- name, city, state, and store type are all null. Nearly a third of the business cannot be attributed to a store.
critical_data_gap.limitation: The feed gives per-location transaction count and basket COGS only -- no revenue or margin by store -- so true location profitability cannot be ranked. Add per-location revenue to enable store-level margin decisions.

data_quality_notes: PROXY MODEL: All revenue/COGS/gross-profit figures are directional proxies (unit_price/unit_cost x weekly appearance count), not booked financials. Use for direction and relative size, not exact P&L.
LOCATION GAP: 1,312 transactions (30.5%) carry null location metadata (LOC_OTHER) -- the top data-quality fix; it blinds any per-store P&L and limits PO splitting by store.
NO PER-STORE REVENUE/MARGIN: The feed exposes per-location transaction count and basket COGS only. Add per-location revenue to enable store-level margin ranking.
PROMO DEPTH MISSING: The feed exposes basket COGS but not revenue or discount depth, so the exact discount giveaway can't be sized -- only its (wrong) direction.
MISCATEGORIZATION: Rice is labeled 'Bakery' in the feed but is a grain/pantry staple -- recategorize so category analytics aren't skewed.
NO SUPPLIER MASTER: raw_metrics.json has no supplier table; supplier names on PO lines are category-based channel guesses and must be confirmed against the products/supplier table before issuing POs.

THRESHOLD ARTIFACTS: Hard cutoffs hid the week's strongest signal -- Diapers missed both the reorder velocity cutoff (by 1 unit) and the spike flag (by 0.54pp). Consider softening hard thresholds or adding a near-miss review band.

operator_action_items.action: Check Cheese (-42%) and Rice (-35%) availability/receiving/spoilage TODAY before the next order.
operator_action_items.why: Staples don't lose 35-42% of demand organically in a week -- this reads as a stockout or supplier miss. Recover the lost volume now; both reports say HOLD the reorder until you know the cause. This is operational lost sales, not weak demand.

operator_action_items.action: Greenlight the selective ~$8,176 PO -- load Diapers (+29%, 225 units), Power Strips, Dish Soap, Shaving Cream, Shampoo, Chips -- and TRIM the eight soft decliners.
operator_action_items.why: Demand softened 3.7% WoW, so this is a smaller, more disciplined buy. The risers are also high-margin (63-70%), so leaning in grows demand and profit together. Override the rule to include Diapers -- it was excluded by 1 unit despite being the week's biggest mover and 6th-highest revenue SKU.

operator_action_items.action: Reprice the cost-inflation outliers: Eggs $4.49 -> $4.79+ (46.6% -> ~50%), Olive Oil $9.99 -> $10.99 (-> ~59%), Ice Cream test $6.29, and Shrimp $16.99 -> $17.99 (-> ~57%).
operator_action_items.why: These sub-56% SKUs are the real margin leaks where commodity cost has outrun shelf price. Eggs is 17pts below your house average -- the clearest fix. Shrimp is special: ordering wants to reorder it (+13.7%) AND margin says raise price, so do both -- rising demand means the increase won't dent volume.

operator_action_items.action: Fix the discount program: ~49% of baskets are discounted with zero measurable lift. Convert blanket discounts to threshold/targeted offers and measure against incremental revenue.
operator_action_items.why: Discounted baskets actually run 2.3% LARGER, meaning the promo rewards customers who'd buy anyway -- a live margin leak on half your transactions. Targeting (unlock above a basket size, or target lapsing customers / slow high-margin SKUs) recovers the giveaway.

operator_action_items.action: Renegotiate Dairy supply as a category and protect durable-goods stock; mix-shift toward high-margin consumables (Chips/Beverage/Produce).
operator_action_items.why: Dairy is your lowest-margin category (54.8%) AND shrinking everywhere -- fix it at the supply level, not SKU by SKU. Separately, 5 durable SKUs carry 51% of gross profit, so a Lawn Mower or Vacuum stockout hurts most -- guard their availability, and grow repeat-purchase margin via front-of-store impulse placement of Chips (70%), Beverage (76%) and Produce (68%).

marco_tldr: Margin's steady at ~63%, but go check why Cheese and Rice fell off a cliff (35-42% drops are stockouts, not lost demand), greenlight the lean ~$8,176 order led by Diapers, nudge Eggs and Olive Oil prices up, and rein in the blanket discounts hitting half your baskets for zero extra sales.
